text.title: Stakeholder Conflict Mediation Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn an erupting conflict in a requirements meeting into a captured decision or a clean escalation.

rule.statement: When two stakeholders openly disagree, the BA MUST verbally pause the broader review within 30 seconds (e.g., "let me pause us here — I want to make sure I capture this disagreement correctly"). Letting the conflict run uncapped past 30 seconds is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Past the 30-second mark, the disagreement turns into argument, audiences harden, and reframe options collapse. Early pause is the only window where neutral framing is still accepted.

rule.statement: The BA MUST reframe the dispute back to a specific clause in the requirements doc, quoted verbatim, and pin the disagreement to one of: (a) interpretation of the clause, (b) priority of the clause, or (c) inclusion of the clause. Mediation does NOT continue until all parties confirm which of (a)(b)(c) is the disagreement.
rule.rationale: Mediating an under-specified disagreement is impossible: stakeholders will keep widening scope. Pinning to a specific clause + a specific (a/b/c) tag is what makes the rest of the loop tractable.

rule.statement: The BA MUST offer exactly three resolution paths: (1) decide here using a quick rubric, (2) defer to an async decision memo with a deadline, (3) escalate to the named project sponsor. The room chooses one before mediation moves further.
rule.rationale: Three named paths force a meta-decision the room CAN make even when the substantive question is contested. Open-ended ("what should we do?") regresses immediately back into the original argument.

rule.statement: Whatever path the room picks, the next step MUST be written into the meeting notes verbatim before review resumes: who does what, by when, against which clause ID. The BA reads the entry aloud and waits for explicit verbal confirmation from each stakeholder in the dispute.
rule.rationale: An unconfirmed verbal "let's do X" decays within 24h; stakeholders later remember different versions. Reading-and-confirming creates a shared, witnessed record before the chemical of resolution wears off.

rule.statement: If the pause-reframe-isolate-propose loop fails to converge after two iterations on the same clause, the BA MUST escalate to the named project sponsor before the meeting ends — without negotiating a third iteration. The escalation message MUST cite both iterations' notes.
rule.rationale: Three+ iterations on the same clause is a strong signal the BA does not have authority to resolve it. Continuing burns the meeting, the BA's credibility, and the rest of the agenda. A hard cap respects the project's escalation contract.

example.code:
[Meeting notes, 10:42]
Maria and Tomás began arguing on the discount-on-subscriptions clause.
BA: "Let me pause — Maria, Tomás, the clause we are on is REQ-114: 'Discount codes are applicable to subscription products.' Is the disagreement about (a) interpretation, (b) priority, or (c) inclusion?"
Both: "(c) inclusion."
BA: "Three paths: decide here with a quick rubric, write a decision memo due Friday, or escalate to Pedro (sponsor). Which?"
Decision: write a memo, owner Maria, due 2026-05-23, escalation if no agreement to Pedro by 2026-05-24.
[Notes read back and confirmed by Maria, Tomás, BA.]
Review resumed at 10:46 on REQ-115.
example.note: Pause within 30s. Clause pinned to (c). Three paths offered. Written, read-back, confirmed. Total mediation: 4 minutes.

example.code:
[Meeting notes, 10:42]
Maria and Tomás argued about subscriptions for 25 minutes. Other attendees got pulled in. We ran out of time and ended without covering REQ-115 to 130.
Action: "the team will resolve it offline."
example.note: No pause, no reframe, no isolation, no path proposal, no written confirm, no escalation. Meeting hostage; agenda lost; "offline" decay guaranteed.
